examples.title: TCP Reno Congestion Control Simulation
examples.description: Simulates the basic TCP Reno algorithm demonstrating Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, and Fast Recovery phases with cwnd (congestion window) dynamics.
examples.code:
import random

class TCPReno:
    def __init__(self, ssthresh=64, initial_cwnd=1):
        self.cwnd = initial_cwnd  # Congestion window
        self.ssthresh = ssthresh  # Slow start threshold
        self.phase = "Slow Start"
        self.rtt = 0
        
    def on_ack(self):
        """Handle incoming ACK - adjusts cwnd based on phase"""
        self.rtt += 1
        
        if self.phase == "Slow Start":
            # Exponential growth: cwnd increases by 1 MSS per ACK
            self.cwnd += 1
            if self.cwnd >= self.ssthresh:
                self.phase = "Congestion Avoidance"
                print(f"RTT {self.rtt}: cwnd={self.cwnd}, phase={self.phase}")
        else:
            # Linear growth: cwnd increases by 1/cwnd per ACK (approx 1 MSS per RTT)
            self.cwnd += 1 / self.cwnd
            
        print(f"RTT {self.rtt}: cwnd={self.cwnd:.2f}, phase={self.phase}")
        return self.cwnd
    
    def on_timeout(self):
        """Timeout event - fall back to slow start"""
        print(f"RTT {self.rtt}: TIMEOUT! ssthresh={self.cwnd//2}, cwnd=1")
        self.ssthresh = max(self.cwnd // 2, 1)
        self.cwnd = 1
        self.phase = "Slow Start"
    
    def on_duplicate_ack(self):
        """3 duplicate ACKs - fast recovery"""
        print(f"RTT {self.rtt}: 3 Duplicate ACKs - Fast Recovery")
        self.ssthresh = self.cwnd // 2
        self.cwnd = self.ssthresh + 3
        self.phase = "Fast Recovery"

# Demonstration
tcp = TCPReno()
print("=== TCP Reno Simulation ===\n")

# Simulate slow start (first 10 ACKs)
print("--- Slow Start Phase ---")
for _ in range(10):
    tcp.on_ack()

# Simulate timeout
print("\n--- Timeout Event ---")
tcp.on_timeout()

# Simulate recovery and congestion avoidance
print("\n--- After Recovery ---")
for _ in range(5):
    tcp.on_ack()
examples.explanation: This simulation demonstrates TCP Reno's three main phases: (1) Slow Start - cwnd grows exponentially (doubles every RTT) until it reaches ssthresh; (2) Congestion Avoidance - cwnd grows linearly (adds ~1 MSS per RTT); (3) Fast Recovery - after 3 duplicate ACKs, cwnd is halved and grows linearly. The code tracks cwnd changes and phase transitions.

examples.title: TCP Variants Comparison: Tahoe vs Reno vs NewReno
examples.description: Compares three classic TCP congestion control variants demonstrating their different responses to packet loss events.
examples.code:
class TCPVariant:
    """Base class for TCP variants"""
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.cwnd = 1
        self.ssthresh = 64
        
    def slow_start(self):
        self.cwnd = min(self.cwnd + 1, self.ssthresh)
        
    def congestion_avoidance(self):
        self.cwnd += 1 / self.cwnd
    
    def handle_loss(self, loss_type, ack_count=0):
        """Override in subclasses"""
        pass

class TCP Tahoe(TCPVariant):
    """TCP Tahoe: Always resets to slow start on any loss"""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("TCP Tahoe")
        
    def handle_loss(self, loss_type, ack_count=0):
        self.ssthresh = max(self.cwnd // 2, 1)
        self.cwnd = 1  # Always fall back to 1 MSS
        return f"{self.name}: Loss={loss_type}, ssthresh={self.ssthresh}, cwnd={self.cwnd}"

class TCPReno(TCPVariant):
    """TCP Reno: Fast recovery on duplicate ACKs"""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("TCP Reno")
        
    def handle_loss(self, loss_type, ack_count=0):
        self.ssthresh = max(self.cwnd // 2, 1)
        if loss_type == "timeout":
            self.cwnd = 1
        else:  # 3 duplicate ACKs
            self.cwnd = self.ssthresh + 3
        return f"{self.name}: Loss={loss_type}, ssthresh={self.ssthresh}, cwnd={self.cwnd}"

class TCPNewReno(TCPVariant):
    """TCP NewReno: Partial ACK handling during fast recovery"""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("TCP NewReno")
        self.recovery_phase = False
        
    def handle_loss(self, loss_type, ack_count=0):
        self.ssthresh = max(self.cwnd // 2, 1)
        if loss_type == "timeout":
            self.cwnd = 1
            self.recovery_phase = False
        elif ack_count == 1:  # First partial ACK in recovery
            self.cwnd = self.ssthresh + 1  # Adjust cwnd
            self.recovery_phase = True
        elif ack_count >= 2:  # All data recovered
            self.cwnd = self.ssthresh
            self.recovery_phase = False
        return f"{self.name}: Loss={loss_type}, acks={ack_count}, cwnd={self.cwnd}"

# Simulation comparison
print("=== TCP Variants Comparison ===\n")
print("Initial cwnd = 16 for all variants\n")

variants = [TCP Tahoe(), TCPReno(), TCPNewReno()]

for variant in variants:
    variant.cwnd = 16
    print(f"--- {variant.name} ---")
    
    # Scenario 1: Timeout
    print(f"Before timeout: cwnd={variant.cwnd}")
    print(variant.handle_loss("timeout"))
    
    # Reset for next test
    variant.cwnd = 16
    
    # Scenario 2: 3 duplicate ACKs
    print(f"Before dup ACK: cwnd={variant.cwnd}")
    print(variant.handle_loss("duplicate_ack"))
    
    # NewReno partial ACK
    if variant.name == "TCP NewReno":
        print(variant.handle_loss("duplicate_ack", ack_count=1))
        print(variant.handle_loss("duplicate_ack", ack_count=2))
    
    print()
examples.explanation: This example compares three classic TCP variants: (1) TCP Tahoe - treats all losses the same, always resets cwnd to 1; (2) TCP Reno - implements fast recovery, halves cwnd on 3 duplicate ACKs; (3) TCP NewReno - improves on Reno by handling partial ACKs during recovery. The simulation shows how each variant responds differently to timeout vs duplicate ACK scenarios.

examples.title: Modern TCP: CUBIC and BBR Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates modern TCP congestion control algorithms - CUBIC (loss-based) and BBR (model-based/bandwidth-delay product), showing their fundamentally different approaches.
examples.code:
import math

class TCPCubic:
    """
    TCP CUBIC: Loss-based congestion control
    Uses cubic function for window growth: W(t) = C(t - K)³ + W_max
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.cwnd = 10
        self.w_max = 10
        self.beta = 0.7
        self.C = 0.4
        self.k = 0
        self.rtt_count = 0
        
    def calculate_k(self):
        """Calculate K - inflection point"""
        self.k = ((self.w_max * self.beta) / self.C) ** (1/3)
        
    def update_wmax(self):
        """Update W_max after loss"""
        self.w_max = self.cwnd
        self.calculate_k()
        
    def grow(self, rtt):
        """CUBIC window growth function"""
        self.rtt_count += 1
        t = self.rtt_count - self.k
        
        # Concave region (below W_max): aggressive growth
        # Convex region (above W_max): probing phase
        w_cubic = self.C * (t ** 3) + self.w_max
        
        # TCP-friendly region (when far from W_max)
        w_tcp = self.cwnd + (3 * self.beta) / (2 - self.beta) * (1 / self.cwnd)
        
        # Use TCP-friendly when below W_max for fairness
        if self.cwnd < self.w_max:
            self.cwnd = max(w_cubic, w_tcp)
        else:
            self.cwnd = w_cubic
            
        return self.cwnd

class TCPBBR:
    """
    TCP BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and RTT):
    Model-based congestion control that estimates bandwidth and RTT
    Sends at rate = min(BtlBw, RrtProp)
    """
    def __init__(self, bandwidth=10, rtt_min=50):
        self.btl_bw = bandwidth  # Bottleneck bandwidth
        self.rtprop = rtt_min    # Minimum RTT
        self.pacing_rate = bandwidth
        self.cwnd = bandwidth * (rtt_min / 1000)  # BDP in packets
        self.cycle_index = 0
        
    def measure_rtt(self, actual_rtt):
        """Update RTprop if new minimum"""
        if actual_rtt < self.rtprop:
            self.rtprop = actual_rtt
            self.update_cwnd()
            
    def update_cwnd(self):
        """Update cwnd based on BDP"""
        bdp = self.btl_bw * (self.rtprop / 1000)
        # BBR targets 2-4 BDP
        self.cwnd = max(bdp * 2, 4)
        
    def cycle_pacing(self):
        """BBR pacing cycle for bandwidth probing"""
        phases = [0.9, 1.0, 1.0, 1.1, 1.1, 1.2, 0.9, 0.9]  # BBR probe phases
        pacing_factor = phases[self.cycle_index % len(phases)]
        self.pacing_rate = self.btl_bw * pacing_factor
        self.cycle_index += 1
        return self.pacing_rate

# Demonstration
print("=== Modern TCP: CUBIC vs BBR ===\n")

# CUBIC simulation
print("--- TCP CUBIC (Loss-Based) ---")
cubic = TCPCubic()
print(f"Initial cwnd: {cubic.cwnd}")
cubic.update_wmax()
print(f"W_max: {cubic.w_max}, K: {cubic.k:.2f}\n")

print("Window growth over 10 RTTs:")
for i in range(1, 11):
    cw = cubic.grow(i)
    print(f"  RTT {i}: cwnd = {cw:.2f}")

# Simulate loss - cwnd drops
print("\n--- Simulated Loss Event ---")
cubic.update_wmax()
cubic.cwnd = cubic.cwnd * 0.7  # Apply beta
print(f"After loss: cwnd = {cubic.cwnd:.2f}, W_max = {cubic.w_max}")

# BBR simulation
print("\n--- TCP BBR (Model-Based) ---")
bbr = TCPBBR(bandwidth=100, rtt_min=40)
print(f"Initial: BtlBw={bbr.btl_bw} Mbps, RTprop={bbr.rtprop}ms, cwnd={bbr.cwnd:.1f} packets")
print(f"BDP = {bbr.btl_bw * bbr.rtprop / 1000:.1f} packets")

print("\nPacing rate cycle (bandwidth probing):")
for i in range(8):
    rate = bbr.cycle_pacing()
    print(f"  Cycle {i+1}: pacing rate = {rate:.1f} Mbps")

print("\n--- Key Differences ---")
print("CUBIC: React to loss, oscillate around link capacity")
print("BBR: Estimate bandwidth/RTT, send at precise rates")
print("CUBIC is loss-based; BBR is model-based")
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates two modern TCP congestion control algorithms: (1) TCP CUBIC - a loss-based algorithm used in Linux default, uses a cubic function for window growth and is designed for high-bandwidth networks; (2) TCP BBR - a model-based algorithm that builds a explicit model of the network path's bandwidth-delay product and paces packets accordingly. The key difference is that CUBIC reacts to packet loss while BBR tries to prevent loss by modeling the network.
